topic: An artist's work is displayed without their permission
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights caught the vibrant swirl of crimson and gold, and my breath hitched in my throat. It was *my* piece, the one I’d poured months of sleepless nights into, now hanging on a wall I didn't recognize. A man with a confident, easy smile stood nearby, gesturing toward the canvas as if it were a universally adored masterpiece. My palms were slick against the cool glass of the display case. Every time he glanced my way, a strange warmth bloomed low in my stomach, tightening my chest until I felt breathless. I wanted to step forward, to demand an explanation, but my feet felt rooted to the polished floor. He looked… pleased. That look, that easy admiration directed at my work, sent a dizzying jolt through me, making my vision swim.

[story 2]

I watched from across the crowded room, a silent observer in my own exhibition. The painting—my vision, my soul laid bare—was being discussed with an air of reverence by critics and patrons. Then, I saw him. He was leaning in close to the piece, his gaze lingering on the texture of the impasto, his expression one of deep, absorbed fascination. A sudden, sharp ache resonated behind my ribs. I felt a desperate urge to move, to intercept him, to speak directly to the source of that captivating attention. My throat felt dry, and I found myself unconsciously smoothing down the fabric of my dress, a nervous tic betraying the frantic rhythm of my pulse. He turned, catching my eye for a fleeting second, and the brief connection felt like a tangible spark, leaving me utterly undone.

[story 3]

The sheer audacity of it made my jaw tighten. My signature piece, the one that represented everything I felt, was being framed, cataloged, and appreciated by strangers. I stood near the entrance, trying to appear detached, but every nerve ending seemed hyper-alert. When the curator approached, speaking with an almost reverent tone about the piece’s ‘untapped potential,’ I could feel a strange, heady rush. It wasn't just about the art; it was the attention focused on *me*, the creator. I watched the way the light played across the colors, and a deep, consuming pull settled in my core. I wanted to be the center of that focus, to command that level of captivated regard, even if it meant stepping into the spotlight I hadn't consciously sought.
